coredata.dc:description: Dual-emission fluorescence (FL) sensing platform could not only facilitate sensitive visual sensing, but also increase detection accuracy through the change of the FL response and FL color. Herein, a novel ratiometric FL sensing platform was fabricated using the dual-ligand-functionalized dual-emission Eu(III) nanomaterial (i.e., Eu-CDs) based on antenna effect-induced dual signal amplification strategy. In the as-prepared nanomaterial, 2-thenoyltrifluoro-acetone was used as the first ligand to sensitize the FL characteristic emission of Eu(III). Interestingly, the use of carbon dots as the second ligand for Eu(III) could not only further enhance the FL emission of Eu(III), but also serve as a reference signal to achieve the ratiometric FL signals for the detection of hydrogen peroxide. The detection mechanism was that the S elements in the thiophene unit could be turned into sulfone groups by oxidation with hydrogen peroxide, which changed the coordination environment of Eu(III), leading to the decrease in FL intensity at 616 nm. Based on the ratiometric FL signals, we realized the real-time monitoring and multicolor visual analysis of hydrogen peroxide with a detection limit of 1.88 μM. The extraordinary detection capability of hydrogen peroxide achieved the distinction of normal cells from cancer cells and the highly selective determination of serum glucose as the model target in hydrogen peroxide-involved enzymatic reaction. This work not only provided a novel path for the fabrication of high-performance ratiometric FL sensing platform by ligand-functionalized dual-emission Eu(III)-based nanomaterial, but also showed the potential feasibility for rational design of the FL sensing platform.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dual-emission fluorescence (FL) sensing platform could not only facilitate sensitive visual sensing, but also increase detection accuracy through the change of the FL response and FL color. Herein, a novel ratiometric FL sensing platform was fabricated using the dual-ligand-functionalized dual-emission Eu(III) nanomaterial (i.e., Eu-CDs) based on antenna effect-induced dual signal amplification strategy. In the as-prepared nanomaterial, 2-thenoyltrifluoro-acetone was used as the first ligand to sensitize the FL characteristic emission of Eu(III). Interestingly, the use of carbon dots as the second ligand for Eu(III) could not only further enhance the FL emission of Eu(III), but also serve as a reference signal to achieve the ratiometric FL signals for the detection of hydrogen peroxide. The detection mechanism was that the S elements in the thiophene unit could be turned into sulfone groups by oxidation with hydrogen peroxide, which changed the coordination environment of Eu(III), leading to the decrease in FL intensity at 616 nm. Based on the ratiometric FL signals, we realized the real-time monitoring and multicolor visual analysis of hydrogen peroxide with a detection limit of 1.88 μM. The extraordinary detection capability of hydrogen peroxide achieved the distinction of normal cells from cancer cells and the highly selective determination of serum glucose as the model target in hydrogen peroxide-involved enzymatic reaction. This work not only provided a novel path for the fabrication of high-performance ratiometric FL sensing platform by ligand-functionalized dual-emission Eu(III)-based nanomaterial, but also showed the potential feasibility for rational design of the FL sensing platform.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, studies on novel fluorescence (FL) materials have received great concern based on lanthanide materials due to their long FL lifetime, large Stokes shift, and well luminescence monochromaticity [1–3]. Therefore, lanthanide materials were widely used in different fields, including bioimaging, FL sensing, optical devices and so on [4,5]. Nevertheless, based on Laporte's selection rule, the f-f transitions of lanthanide ions were forbidden, leading to very weak emission [6]. To overcome the drawbacks, organic ligands were applied in the fabrication of lanthanide materials to enhance the FL emission of lanthanide ions through the energy transfer from organic ligands to lanthanide ions, which was called antenna effect [7,8]. Usually, the organic molecules with β-diketone structures were used as ligands to coordinate lanthanide ions and sensitize lanthanide ions through the antenna effect [9]. However, single ligand-based lanthanide materials often suffered from unnoticeable FL intensity and poor stability in water solution [10], because it was difficult to meet the saturated coordination number of lanthanide ions, which might lead to water molecules involved in the coordinating.

The dual-ligand system was one of the facile and efficient strategies to solve the above problems [11,12]. In the dual ligand system, the introduction of a second ligand for coordinating lanthanide ions could supersede water molecules involved in the coordinating and improve the stability and FL intensity of lanthanide materials [13,14]. More importantly, the second ligand could play a vital role in the design of an FL sensing platform with dual emission under single excitation [15,16]. In comparison with the single emitting lanthanide materials, dual-emitting lanthanide materials could not only facilitate sensitive visual sensing but also increase detection accuracy through the change of the FL response and FL color [17]. Accordingly, it was a promising aspect to design a novel FL sensing platform based on double ligand-based lanthanide materials with outstanding ratiometric FL intensity and favorable stability for multicolor visual sensing.

In comparison with other nanocarbons (e.g., carbon nanotubes and carbon nanosphere), carbon dots (CDs) presented simple synthetic routes, tunable functionalities and ultrafine sizes, which had gained significant attention [18]. In addition, benefiting from the stable physicochemical properties, good biocompatibility, low cost and interesting FL properties, CDs were widely applied as a sensor for the visualization detection of various analytes, such as biological macromolecules, small organic molecules and metal ions [19–22]. Notably, there were many carboxylic acid groups on the surface of CDs, which could combine with the metal ions [23]. Inspired by these, in this work, CDs served as the second ligand for Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 to fabricate dual-ligand-functionalized dual-emission Eu(III) nanomaterial (i.e., Eu-CDs). It was found that the characteristic FL of CDs could be used as a reference signal to achieve multicolor visual sensing, and CDs could further sensitize the FL signal of Eu(III). The noticeable variation in the FL color of the proposed sensing platform was integrated with a smartphone-assisted analysis device for multicolor visual analysis of hydrogen peroxide ( Scheme 1). The mechanism and the analytical performance of hydrogen peroxide detection were further discussed. As a proof of concept, the proposed FL sensing platform with facile operation, short analysis time as well as excellent quantification ability had been applied for real-time monitoring and multicolor visual analysis of hydrogen peroxide in the inflammatory process and enzymatic reaction. Our work extended the scope of the dual-ligand-functionalized Eu(III) nanomaterial-based FL sensing platform, and opened up new possibilities for real-time monitoring and multicolor visual analysis of hydrogen peroxide based on antenna effect-induced dual signal amplification strategy.

All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Phorbol-12-myristate-13-acetate (PMA), Pluronic F-127 (F127) and 2-thenoyltrifluoro-acetone (TTA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. (St. Louis, USA). MgCl2, CaCl2, ascorbic acid, citric acid and glucose oxidase were acquired from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). EuCl3, FeCl3, CuCl2, AlCl3, PbCl2, Na2SO4, NaAc, NaC2O4, uric acid, bilirubin, L-cysteine, glucose, glutamine and homocysteine were purchased from Aladdin Shanghai Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). H2O2, NaCl, NaOH, ethanol, NaNO3 and KCl were obtained from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Guangzhou, China). Urea was acquired from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Deionized water (18.2 MU cm, Milli Q, Millipore, Barnstead, CA, USA) from a Millipore water purification system was used throughout the experiments.

FL spectra were recorded by an LS55 FL Spectrometer (PerkinElmer Enspire, UK) with a scanning rate of 250 nm/min. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected on a Nicolet iS5 FTIR (Thermo, USA). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were acquired on a JEOL JEM-F200 TEM (JEOL, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were collected on a Thermo Scientific Nexsa (Thermo, USA). Zeta potentials were obtained via a Malvern Zetasizer Nano MAL (Malvern, UK). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were measured using Bruker D8 Advance (Bruker, Germany). X-ray diﬀractometer equipped with graphite-monochromatized Cu Kα radiation (λ= 1.5406 Å). And a 2θ angle of the diﬀractometer was stepped from 5° to 90° at a scan rate of 0.02°/s. The FL lifetime and quantum yield were measured by FLS1000 spectroﬂuorometer (Edinburgh, UK). For FL lifetime, the sample was excited at 375 nm with a nanosecond ﬂash lamp. An integrating sphere was used for quantum yield.

The CDs were synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis method [24]. Briefly, 0.8 g of citric acid and 1.6 g of urea were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. Then the as-prepared solution was heated in a 700 W microwave oven for 15 min. After cooling to room temperature, the dark brown solid was obtained and dispersed in 100 mL of deionized water. Subsequently, an ultrasound was performed for 30 min. The product was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. After filtering through a 0.45 μm filter, the solution was freeze-dried for further purification. The original CDs (10 mg) powders were dispersed in 1 mL of ethanol. Then an ultrasound was performed for 30 min. After filtering through a 0.45 μm filter, the solution was dried by rotation-evaporation. Finally, the dark yellow powders were collected for further use.

The Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 was synthesized with the Eu/TTA (S) atomic fraction ratio almost three times according to the literature method [25]. Briefly, 0.666 g of TTA was thoroughly dissolved in 7 mL of ethanol under stirring at 40 ℃. After the TTA solution was cooled to room temperature, 3 mL of NaOH (1 M) solution was added into the TTA solution under stirring for 20 min to adjust the pH value to approximately 7. Thereafter, 0.258 g of EuCl3 was dissolved in 7 mL of deionized water and added dropwise to the mixture solution under stirring. Subsequently, an opalescent solution was obtained and heated at 60 ℃ under reflux for 3 h, and then cooled by using the ice water. It should be noted that the whole reaction process was carried out under a nitrogen atmosphere. The precipitates were collected by centrifugation, washed with both deionized water and ethanol three times and dried at 55 ℃ for 24 h. Finally, a pale yellow sample was obtained as Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 for further use.

The Eu-CDs were synthesized by simply mixing and heating two raw materials. Typically, 205 mg of Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 and 55 mg of CDs were dissolved in 2.5 mL of ethanol and kept at 80 ℃ under reflux for 5 h. The above product was dialyzed against ethanol for 24 h with a cellulose ester membrane bag (Mw = 1000) to remove the excess precursors. Finally, Eu-CDs were obtained after vacuum drying to remove the ethanol.

The following procedure was applied to synthesize Eu-CDs@F127 [26]. Ethanol (2 mL) was used as the solvent for the dissolution of Eu-CDs (1 mg) and F127 (10 mg). Then ethanol was removed by vacuum drying at 55 ℃ for 48 h. 1 mL of deionized water was subsequently added to the dried mixture and stirred for 30 min at room temperature to obtain the stable dispersed Eu-CDs@F127 nanoparticles.

A 1 mg/mL stock solution of Eu-CDs was prepared by dissolving 1 mg of Eu-CDs in 1 mL of ethanol. The working solution of Eu-CDs was freshly prepared by diluting the Eu-CDs stock solution with ethanol. 700 μL of Eu-CDs working solution was mixed with 300 μL of H2O2 working solution. Then the mixture was shaken at 37 ℃ for 30 min. The FL spectrum and FL image were obtained under an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The FL intensity at 616 nm and 515 nm was recorded as F616 and F515, respectively. The inhibition ratio of FL intensity of the proposed system was described as (I0-I)/I0, where I0 and I were the FL intensity ratio of F616/F515 in the absence and in the presence of H2O2. The FL image was analyzed by using the color picker on the smartphone to obtain the RGB intensity.

Firstly, 100 μL of glucose solution and 50 μL of glucose oxidase (600 μg/mL) were mixed and shaken at 37 ℃ for 20 min. Then, the mixture was diluted with deionized water to 300 μL. 100 μL of the diluted mixture solution was added into 900 μL of Eu-CDs working solution (7.5 μg/mL). After the solution was shaken at 37 ℃ for 30 min, the FL spectrum and FL image were obtained under an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The FL intensity at 616 nm and 515 nm was recorded as F616 and F515, respectively. The inhibition ratio of FL intensity of the proposed system was described as (I0-I)/I0, where I0 and I were the FL intensity ratio of F616/F515 in the absence and in the presence of glucose. The FL image was analyzed by using the color picker on the smartphone to obtain the RGB intensity.

The human serum samples were obtained from The Affiliated Qingyuan Hospital of Guangzhou Medical University (IRB-2024–019). 5 μL of each human serum sample was diluted to an appropriate concentration with deionized water. The diluted human serum samples were analyzed immediately by the proposed method.

In the cytotoxicity assay, the cells (104/well) were cultured in a 96-well plate for 24 h. Then, the medium was replaced by 100 µL of DMEM medium containing different concentrations of Eu-CDs@F127 nanoparticles (i.e., 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6 mg/mL). After 12 h of incubation, the cells were washed with DMEM medium, and 100 μL of DMEM containing CCK-8 (10 μL) was added to each well. The cells were further incubated for 1 h. The absorbance at 450 nm was measured on a Multimode Plate Readers (Tecan Infinite 200pro).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of hydrogen peroxide in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: PMA was used to stimulate the formation of endogenous H2O2 in living cells. Real-time determination of PMA-stimulated H2O2 was achieved by in-situ FL measurement. The cells were seeded into a 12-well plate at a density of 2 × 106 cells per well. After the cells were incubated for 12 h with 5 % CO2 at 37 ℃, the culturing medium was removed. Then 500 µL of the fresh DMEM medium containing Eu-CDs@F127 (5 mg/mL) and PMA (20 ng/mL) was added into the 12-well plates, and the FL intensity at 616 nm and 515 nm were continuously monitored at 10-minute intervals for 1 h and recorded as F616 and F515, respectively. Finally, the difference of the FL intensity ratio in the proposed system was described as I0-I, where I0 and I were the FL intensity ratio of F616/F515 in the absence and in the presence of PMA.

Dual-ligand-functionalized dual-emission nanomaterial (i.e., Eu-CDs) were fabricated by a facile and simple refluxing approach ( Fig. 1A). As shown in Fig. 1B, Eu-CDs without any lattice fringes were well-dispersed and spherical in shape with particle sizes in the range of 2.5–4.5 nm. The result revealed that Eu-CDs had an amorphous structure and may be consisted of aggregated or cross-linked polymers [27]. The average particle size of Eu-CDs was 3.27 nm (Fig. 1C), which was similar to that of CDs (i.e., 3.34 nm) (Fig. S1). The results indicated that there was no obvious change in the morphology of CDs after modification by Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 [28]. In addition, the XRD pattern (Fig. S2) showed that Eu-CDs exhibited no characteristic peaks, confirming that Eu-CDs with an amorphous structure were formed. The result was consistent with the widely reported CDs [29–31].

To further confirm the modification of Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 onto the surface of CDs, XPS analysis and FT-IR spectra were performed. Survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 1D) demonstrated the presence of Eu 3d3/2, Eu 3d5/2, F 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s, C 1 s, and S 2p peaks centered at 1165.1, 1135.1, 688.1, 532.0, 400.3, 285.3 and 165.3 eV, respectively [32,33]. The surface atomic fractions of Eu-CDs were listed in Table S1. It should be noted that the existence of Eu and S in Eu-CDs with an atomic fraction of 1:3 was consistent with that in the precursor Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2, which implied the existence of Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 in CDs. On the other hand, In O 1 s spectra (Fig. S3A), the peak CO shifted from 531.4 eV to 531.6 eV and the peak C-O shifted from 532.5 eV to 533.0 eV after CDs coordinated with Eu(III) as the second ligand. Meanwhile, the peaks at 1164.4 and 1135.0 eV were associated with Eu 3d3/2 and Eu 3d5/2 in Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2, respectively (Fig. S3B). The peaks decreased to 1164.1 and 1134.7 eV in Eu-CDs. The results revealed the strong coordination interaction between Eu(III) and carboxyl groups in CDs [16,34]. Furthermore, the absorption peaks at 1605, 1543 and 1137 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 (Fig. 1E) corresponded to the CO stretching vibration of the carbonyl part of β-diketone, CC stretching vibration and the C-F stretching vibration, respectively [35,36]. Meanwhile, the absorption peak at 1709 cm−1 was attributed to the CO stretching vibration of CDs [37]. All these bands were also observed for Eu-CDs, which implied that Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 were successfully modified onto the surface of CDs. It could be found that the absorption peak assigned to the CO group was blue-shifted and the intensity was weakened significantly when Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 was combined with CDs. The result indicated that the free CO groups could coordinate with Eu(III) and served as an antenna ligand for sensitizing the FL of Eu(III) [38].

Additionally, the CDs exhibited a highly negative zeta potential of −32.23 mV (Fig. S4), which might be attributed to a large number of -COOH groups with negative charge on the surface of CDs [39]. However, after the formation of Eu-CDs, the zeta potential turned to 4.97 mV. The phenomenon was ascribed to the incorporation of positively charged Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2, which suggested that the introduction of Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 was also realized by electrostatic adsorption on the surface of CDs [40]. All the results indicated that Eu-CDs were successfully fabricated, which would be further used for the fabrication of a fluorescent sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical property of dual-ligand-functionalized dual-emission nanomaterial
ce:section.ce:para: In order to realize the rapid and simple multicolor visual analysis of H2O2, we first tested the optical properties of Eu-CDs. The UV-Vis absorption spectra of Eu-CDs, Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 and CDs were shown in Fig. 2A. Eu-CDs showed three absorption peaks that appeared at 270, 342 and 410 nm. The last absorption peak was derived from CDs [41], while the other two absorption peaks were assigned to Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 [42,43], which further indicated that Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 was successfully combined with CDs. On the one hand, when the excitation wavelength was changed from 330 nm to 410 nm, the FL intensity of CDs gradually increased, and there was a slight red shift in the emission peak (Fig. 2B). The observed excitation dependent emission spectra were attributed to complex surface groups on the surface of CDs [44]. The same phenomenon also occurs in the FL spectra of Eu-CDs (Fig. S5). On the other hand, the effect of the purification steps on the optical property of CDs was evaluated by using FL spectra. The dialyzed CDs were dialyzed using a 1000 Da membrane after filtering through a 0.22 μm membrane [45]. As shown in Fig. S6, FL excitation spectra and FL emission spectra of CDs and dialyzed CDs were almost the same. The observed excitation dependent emission spectra of dialyzed CDs were also the same as those of the original CDs. Note that the concentration of the original CDs was lower than that of the dialyzed CDs, which implied the excellent optical property of the original CDs.

As shown in Fig. 2C and Fig. S7, under 343 nm excitation, four sharp emission peaks located at 537, 581, 593, and 616 nm were ascribed to the 5D1→7F1, 5D0→7F0, 5D0→7F1, and 5D0→7F2, respectively [34,46]. At the same time, the broad and weak emission peak located at 515 nm was assigned to CDs. In the excitation spectra of Eu-CDs and Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2, the broad band centered at about 343 nm, while the weak peak located at about 407 nm in the excitation spectrum of Eu-CDs was assigned to the CDs (Fig. S8). Note that after carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs were coordinated with Eu(III), the FL emission from Eu(III) at 616 nm was greatly enhanced (Fig. 2D). The possible reason for the phenomenon was that CDs as the second ligand for Eu(III) could prevent the nonradiative transition of Eu(III) excited states to H2O [6]. In addition, as shown in Figs. 2E and 2F, the FL decay curves were well fitted with single exponential decay function. As listed in Table S2, the FL lifetime of Eu-CDs under 616 nm emission was longer than that of Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2; and its FL lifetime under 515 nm emission was shorter than that of CDs. Meanwhile, the FL quantum yield of Eu-CDs (i.e., 8.64 %) was higher than that of Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 (i.e., 4.10 %). These results implied that introducing CDs as a second antenna ligand could reduce the coordination between Eu(III) and H2O, and thus fundamentally promoted the energy transfer from CDs to Eu(III), ultimately further improving the FL signal of Eu-CDs [14,47].

Moreover, the ratiometric FL response of Eu-CDs hardly changed under different days (Fig. S9), indicating that Eu-CDs had satisfactory photostability. Interestingly, when H2O2 was added into the Eu-CDs solution, the intensity of the emission peak located at 616 nm was decreased, while the intensity at 515 nm remained unchanged. Simultaneously, the color of Eu-CDs solution was turned from red to green under UV light at wavelength 365 nm (inset of Fig. 3A). Accordingly, we fabricated the FL sensing platform based on Eu-CDs with dual-ligand-functionalized dual-emission. Subsequently, more experiments would be performed in the following sections to explore the potential application and mechanism of the proposed fluorescent sensing platform based on Eu-CDs for the real-time monitoring and multicolor visual analysis of H2O2 in the inflammatory process and enzymatic reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of fluorescent sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performance of the proposed fluorescent sensing platform was further investigated by using H2O2. To obtain a more excellent performance for multicolor visual analysis of H2O2, we optimized the concentration of Eu-CDs, the volume ratio of Eu-CDs to H2O2, and reaction time by monitoring the changes in FL intensity and color of the detection system for the detection of different concentrations of H2O2. As shown in Fig. S10-S12, sharp visual color change and good linear correlation between I0-I/I0 and concentration of H2O2 was obtained when the concentration of Eu-CDs was 7.5 μg/mL, volume ratio of Eu-CDs to H2O2 was 7:3 and reaction time was 30 min.

Under the above optimum experimental conditions, Fig. 3A displayed that the FL intensity of the proposed FL sensing system at 616 nm decreased with the increasing concentration of H2O2. In addition, the FL intensity at 515 nm was almost unchanged. Meanwhile, the FL color of the system changed from red to green (inset of Fig. 3A). Fig. 3B showed the inhibition ratio of FL intensity was proportional to the H2O2 concentration in the range from 0 to 1.0 mM. The regression equation was Y = 0.2850X + 0.0141 (R2 = 0.9890). In addition, there was another linear relationship between the inhibition ratio of FL intensity and the H2O2 concentration in the range from 1.0 to 10 mM (Fig. 3C). The regression equation was Y = 0.0486X + 0.2665 (R2 = 0.9893). In the above equations, X was the H2O2 concentration and Y was described as (I0-I)/I0, where I0 and I were the FL intensity ratio of F616/F515 in the absence and in the presence of H2O2.

On the other hand, the obvious color change from red to green provided an important basis for visual analysis (Figs. 3D and 3E). The FL images were analyzed by using the color picker on the smartphone to obtain the RGB intensity. As illustrated in Fig. 3D, there was a favorable linear relationship between the value of R/B and the H2O2 concentration in the range from 0 to 10 mM. The corresponding linear equation was Y = -0.0441X + 0.9355 (R2 = 0.9973), where X was the H2O2 concentration and Y was the value of R/B. The detection limit for H2O2 determination through the proposed FL sensing platform (S/N = 3) was 1.88 μM. The relative standard deviation for twenty times repeated measurements of 1 mM H2O2 was 1.86 % (Fig. S13). Overall, the FL sensing platform could be used for the sensitive detection and visual analysis of H2O2, with better performance compared with the earlier reported visual FL methods (Table S3) [48,49]. Note that we also tested the analytical performance of Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 for the detection of H2O2. As shown in Fig. S14, the FL signals of Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 at 616 nm decreased with the increasing concentration of H2O2. However, there was little change in FL color of Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2 solution after incubation with H2O2. Moreover, the inhibition ratio of FL signals at 616 nm was only proportional to the H2O2 concentration in the range from 0 to 1.0 mM. The results implied that the Eu-CDs exhibited better analytical performance for H2O2 detection in comparison with Eu(TTA)3(H2O)2, which further verified the advantage of the dual-ligand-functionalized dual-emission Eu(III) nanomaterial based on antenna effect-induced dual signal amplification strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of fluorescent sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: To clarify the sensing mechanism of the proposed FL sensing platform system, we analyzed the XPS spectra of Eu-CDs before and after reacting with H2O2. As shown in Fig. 4A, the O 1 s spectrum in Eu-CDs displayed two peaks at 532.96 and 531.63 eV were corresponding to C-O and CO respectively [50]. Remarkably, after the reaction between Eu-CDs and H2O2, the OSO bond with the O 1 s binding energy of 531.91 eV was observed in Fig. 4 C [51]. In addition, the peak of the S 2p spectrum centered at 168.10 eV was originated from C-SOx-C (Fig. 4B) [52]. The other two peaks centered at 165.30 and 164.19 eV attributed to S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 peaks of C-S-C in the thiophene structure, respectively [53]. Moreover, the content of C-SOx-C significantly increased and a new peak assigned to C-SOx-C appeared at 169.54 eV (Fig. 4D) after the reaction between Eu-CDs and H2O2. The results suggested the formation of OSO bonds on the surface of Eu-CDs (Fig. 4E) [54]. Note that the binding energy of Eu 4d decreased after the reaction (Fig. S15), indicating SO groups donated p-electrons to Eu(III). The electron-transfer process could change the coordination environment of Eu(III), leading to a decrease in the FL intensity of Eu(III) [55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of hydrogen peroxide in enzymatic reaction
ce:section.ce:para: It was well-known that H2O2 was a byproduct of enzymatic reaction. Since H2O2 could be readily produced from glucose by using glucose oxidase as the catalyst [56], glucose was selected as the model target. Glucose oxidase was added to the proposed FL sensing system. As shown in Fig. 5A, with the increasing concentration of glucose, the FL intensity of the proposed FL sensing system at 616 nm decreased while the FL peak at 515 nm remained almost unchanged. At the same time, the FL color of the proposed sensing system changed from red to light green under 365 nm UV light with the increasing glucose concentration ranging from 0 to 8 mM (inset of Fig. 5A). These phenomena were consistent with those in the detection of H2O2. Under the optimal experimental conditions (Fig. S16-S20), the inhibition ratio of FL intensity was linearly correlated with the concentration of glucose in the range of 0–1 mM (R2 = 0.9943) and exhibited an excellent linear relationship with the logarithm of glucose concentration in the range of 1–8 mM (R2 = 0.9966) (Figs. 5B and 5 C). The result showed that the value of R/B was also linearly correlated with the logarithm of glucose concentration from 0 to 8 mM (R2 = 0.9957) (Fig. 5D). The detection limit for glucose detection was estimated to be 1.26 μM (S/N = 3).

Subsequently, the color of the proposed sensing system in the presence of glucose with different concentrations in the CIE chromaticity coordinates (Fig. 5E) was in accordance with that recognized by the bare eyes under 365 nm UV light. The relative standard deviation for twenty repeated measurements of 1 mM glucose was 1.36 % (Fig. S21), indicating that the proposed FL sensing platform had good stability and reproducibility for glucose detection.

To evaluate the specificity of the proposed sensing system for the detection of glucose in human serum samples, we assessed the effects of several possibly interfering species in human serum samples, including K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Ac-, SO4 2-, C2O4 2-, NO3 -, urea, homocysteine, glutamine, cysteine, bilirubin, ascorbic acid and uric acid. Briefly, a sample solution containing glucose and different concentrations of the individual possibly interfering species was analyzed by the proposed sensing system (Fig. S22). The common concentration ranges of these possibly interfering species in human serum samples were also listed in Table S4. A compound was considered as non-interfering if the variation of the inhibition ratio of FL intensity of the proposed system was less than ± 5 % in comparison with that obtained in the absence of the referred compound [57]. The results revealed that these possibly interfering species had no influence on the determination of glucose, indicating that the proposed method presented high selectivity for the determination of glucose in human serum samples. To support the potential of the proposed sensing system in enzymatic reaction, the concentrations of glucose in human serum samples were detected. The results were summarized in Table S5. The analytical merits of the proposed sensing system were tested in comparison with the clinical method, which was performed in The Affiliated Qingyuan Hospital of Guangzhou Medical University. The recoveries ranged from 94.48 % to 99.93 %, indicating that the two methods were in good agreement. The results suggested that the proposed FL sensing system was applicable to the analysis of glucose in the clinical samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of hydrogen peroxide in the inflammatory process
ce:section.ce:para: Inflammation played a critical role in different stages of cancer development, including tumorigenesis, metastasis and invasion [58]. The excessive production of H2O2 by inflammatory cells may induce the mutations of neighboring cells and cause neurodegeneration disease or cancer. Therefore, it is necessary to in situ real-time detection of H2O2 released from cells in the inflammatory process. As a proof of concept of the application of the proposed FL sensing platform, the as-prepared Eu-CDs@F127 was used in situ real-time detection of H2O2 released from cancer cells and normal cells (i.e., MCF-7 cells and HPDE cells). Firstly, the cytotoxicity of Eu-CDs@F127 to the above two types of live cells was investigated by CCK-8 assay. As shown in Fig. 6A and 6B, the cell viability for HPDE cells and MCF-7 cells maintained above 80 % after incubation with 6 mg/mL Eu-CDs@F127 for 12 h, indicating that Eu-CDs@F127 in these working concentrations was nearly nontoxic. In addition, F127 possessed a balanced hydrophilic-hydrophobic nature and excellent biocompatibility, which could improve the water solubility of Eu-CDs [59]. Moreover, there was little difference in the maximum excitation wavelength and maximum emission wavelength between Eu-CDs and Eu-CDs@F127 (Fig. S23). Meanwhile, the FL signals of Eu(III) were slightly improved after addition of F127. Accordingly, Eu-CDs@F127 could be used for the detection of H2O2 in biological environment. Furthermore, the cancer cells and normal cells were cultured with the same approach and stimulated with PMA to produce H2O2. Since the amount of H2O2 produced by PMA stimulation in normal cells was limited compared with cancer cells, the H2O2 content in normal cells was lower than that in cancer cells. Consequently, the FL intensity ratio F616/F515 of Eu-CDs-F127 decreased to a lesser extent in normal cells compared with that in cancer cells. (Fig. 6C). Accordingly, our system can be used to distinguish the H2O2 levels between cancer cells and normal cells.

In summary, we successfully designed a facile and simple strategy to fabricate a novel ratiometric FL sensing platform. The design was based on dual-ligand-functionalized Eu-CDs with dual-emission, in which carbon dots as the second ligand could further enhance the FL emission of Eu(III) through the antenna effect and serve as a reference signal to achieve the ratiometric FL signals. Importantly, when hydrogen peroxide was added into the Eu-CDs solution, the FL signals at 616 nm from Eu(III) could significantly decrease due to the formation of sulfone groups in Eu-CDs. On the contrary, the FL signal at 515 nm from CDs was unchanged. Accordingly, we realized the real-time monitoring and multicolor visual analysis of hydrogen peroxide by using the ratiometric FL signals. The proposed FL sensing platform had been successfully used for the detection of hydrogen peroxide in the inflammatory process and enzymatic reaction. The reasonable design of dual-ligand-functionalized dual-emission Eu-CDs would provide a new prospect for the application of lanthanide nanocomplexes in biosensors and bioimaging fields.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Lingyan Gui received a BS degree from Chongqing Medical University in 2022. She is currently studying for a master's degree at The Affiliated Qingyuan Hospital of Guangzhou Medical University. Her research interest focuses on the synthesis of fluorescence biosensors for clinical diagnosis.

Xu Teng received her PhD degree from Beijing University of Chemical Technology in 2015. She currently is an associate research fellow at The Affiliated Qingyuan Hospital of Guangzhou Medical University. Her research interests focus on the design of fluorescence, chemiluminescence, and electrochemiluminescence biosensors.

Ye Yang received a BS degree from Gannan Medical University. Currently, she studies at Zhejiang Sci-Tech University as a postgraduate student. Her research interests focus on biomarker detection by novel fluorescence nanomaterials.
Tao Liu received his master's degree in 2024 from Dali University. His research interests focuse on isothermal amplification development and fluorescence biosensors.

Yaru Liang received her PhD degree from Sun Yat-sen University in 2017. She currently is a postgraduate tutor at The Affiliated Qingyuan Hospital of Guangzhou Medical University. She is mainly engaged in the clinical application of fluorescence biosensors.
Jun Zeng is currently a professor at The Affiliated Qingyuan Hospital of Guangzhou Medical University. His research interests focuse on fluorescence biosensors.

Xianying Zhang received her BS degree from the Guangdong University of Foreign Studies in 2021, she currently is a laboratory manager at The Affiliated Qingyuan Hospital of Guangzhou Medical University. Her research interests focus on the preparation of fluorescent nanomaterials.

Linhai Li is currently a professor in clinical laboratory diagnosis at The Affiliated Qingyuan Hospital of Guangzhou Medical University. His research interests focuse on fluorescence biosensors for clinical laboratory diagnosis.
